---
<<<<<<< HEAD
title: "Wolf"
=======
title: "Wolf abundance"
>>>>>>> 696d3711 (update wolf: new ID name, upscaling method and figure)
subtitle: |
  [NO_WOLF_001]
indicatorID: 
  - NO_WOLF_001
indicatorName: 
<<<<<<< HEAD
  - Wolf abundance
=======
  - Wolf abundance [Ulvebestand]
>>>>>>> 696d3711 (update wolf: new ID name, upscaling method and figure)
verbatimName: 
  - Ulvebestand
folderName: NO_WOLF_001
country: Norway
continent: Europe
ECT: B3 Functional state characteristics
Realm: 
  - Terrestrial (T) # e.g. Freshwater (F)
Biome: 
  - T2 Temperate-boreal forests and woodlands biome
Ecosystem: 
  - T2.1 Boreal/montane forests
<<<<<<< HEAD
verbatimEcosystem: boreal/subalpin skog
=======
>>>>>>> 696d3711 (update wolf: new ID name, upscaling method and figure)
yearAdded: 2026 # the year when the indicator was first submitted to ecRxiv
yearLastUpdate: 2026
status: incomplete # options are complete, incomplete, or deprecated
Version: "1.0.0" # See wiki page: https://github.com/NINAnor/ecRxiv/wiki#versioning
VersionComment: First version on ecRxiv; indicator described in the Norwegian Nature Index
url: https://github.com/NINAnor/ecRxiv/tree/main/indicators/NO_WOLF_001
Normalised: Yes
<<<<<<< HEAD
SpatialAggregationPathway: Aggregate (population model, large-carnivore management region) - [Scale - Truncate] - Downscale (municipalities) - Aggregate (geographic region) - Aggregate (national level)
Precision: Approximate 95% Monte Carlo interval (2.5th–97.5th percentile of 1000 resampled area-weighted means) as inferential uncertainty around the point estimate (median).
=======
SpatialAggregationPathway: Aggregate (population model, large-carnivore management region) - [Scale - Truncate] - Aggregate (national level)
>>>>>>> 696d3711 (update wolf: new ID name, upscaling method and figure)
format: 
  html:
    embed-resources: true
    code-fold: true
    toc: true
    toc-title: Contents
    toc-depth: 3
    smooth-scroll: true
execute: 
  cache: true
author:
  - name: Aud H. Halbritter
    email: aud.halbritter@uib.no
    affiliations:
<<<<<<< HEAD
      - name: University of Bergen
  - name: Erik Framstad
    affiliations:
      - name: Norwegian Institute for Nature Research
  - name: Katrine Eldegard
    affiliations:
      - name: Norwegian University of Life Sciences
AuthorList: Halbritter, A. H., Framstad, E., Eldegard, K.
=======
      - id: myID
        name: University of Bergen
AuthorList: Halbritter, A. H.
>>>>>>> 696d3711 (update wolf: new ID name, upscaling method and figure)
date: May 15, 2026
data_availability: gold
code_reproducibility: gold
open_science_badge: gold
callout-icon: false
lightbox: true
css: ../../../style.css
code-links:
      - text: Add a review
        icon: github
        href: https://github.com/NINAnor/ecRxiv
hide: FALSE # Change to FALSE
bibliography: references.bib
---

<!--# This is a template for how to document the indicator analyses. Make sure also to not change the order, or modify, the headers, unless you really need to. This is because it easier to read if all the indicators are presented using the same layout. If there is one header where you don't have anything to write, just leave the header as is, and don't write anything below it. If you are providing code, be careful to annotate and comment on every step in the analysis. Before starting it is recommended to fill in as much as you can in the metadata file. This file will populate the initial table in your output.-->

<!--# Load all you dependencies here -->

```{r setup}
#| include: false
#| cache: false
library(knitr)
library(tidyverse)
library(kableExtra)
library(gt)
library(here)
library(anybadger)
library(yaml)
library(tibble)
library(conflicted)
# weird workarounds for cache issues
conflicts_prefer(dplyr::filter)
conflicts_prefer(dplyr::lag)
conflicts_prefer(dplyr::select)
pull<-dplyr::pull
library(NIcalc)
library(ecTools)
library(MetBrewer)

# This workflow uses `renv`. Please run `renv::restore()` in order to reproduce the working environment and run the analysis.
# renv::restore()

knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE)
```

```{r source}
#| echo: false
#| cache: false
source(here::here("_common.R"))
```



<!--# The following parts are autogenerated. Do not edit. -->

::: {layout-ncol="6"}

```{r statusBadge}
#| echo: false
status_badge(as.character(st))
```

```{r versionBadge}
#| echo: false
version_badge(my_version_number = as.character(version[[1]]))
```

```{r dataBadge}
#| echo: false
data_badge(as.character(badges[[1]]))
```

```{r codeBadge}
#| echo: false
code_badge(as.character(badges[[2]]))
```

```{r openScienceBadge}
#| echo: false
open_science_badge(as.character(badges[[3]]))
```

```{r licenseBadge}
#| echo: false
# The following inserts a badge specifying the GPL GNU version 3 license. ecRxiv recommends using this license, but you can decide to use another. Contact ecRxiv for help in insert a badge for a different license
license_badge()
```


::: 
 

> **Recommended citation**: `r paste(auth, " ", year, ". ", name, " (ID(s): ", folder_name, ") ", "v. ", version, ". ecRxiv: ", url, sep="")`



::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
## Metadata
```{r tbl-meta2}
#| tbl-cap: 'Indicator metadata'
#| echo: false
#| warning: false
meta |>
  dplyr::filter(Variable %in% c(
    "Indicator ID",
    "Indicator Name",
<<<<<<< HEAD
    "verbatimName",
=======
>>>>>>> 696d3711 (update wolf: new ID name, upscaling method and figure)
    "Continent",
    "Country",
    "Ecosystem Condition Typology Class", 
    "Realm",
    "Biome",
    "Ecosystem",
<<<<<<< HEAD
    "verbatimEcosystem",
=======
>>>>>>> 696d3711 (update wolf: new ID name, upscaling method and figure)
    "Year added", 
    "Last update",
    "Version",
    "Version comment",
    "Normalised",
<<<<<<< HEAD
    "Spatial aggregation pathway",
    "Precision")) |>   
=======
    "Spatial aggregation pathway")) |>   
>>>>>>> 696d3711 (update wolf: new ID name, upscaling method and figure)
  kable()

```
:::

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}

## Log

<!--# Update this logg with short messages for each update -->
- May 15, 2026 - Initial draft for ecRxiv
:::


<hr />

<!--# Document you work below.  -->

## 1. Summary

<<<<<<< HEAD
1. **Background and rationale**:
The wolf (*Canis lupus*) is an adaptable species that thrives across a diversity of environments, including forest ecosystems [@ciucci2026wolf].
As an apex predator, the wolf influences the behavior and distribution of ungulates; by potentially reducing the number of these large herbivores, it exerts top-down control that can lower browsing pressure and thereby promote vegetation recovery.
In Norway, the species currently occurs in low numbers – which is largely due to a strict zoning management policy that utilizes license hunting and active removal to prevent the species from establishing itself outside the wolf zone – and is found mainly in southeastern parts of the country along the border with Sweden.
The indicator is part of the Norwegian Nature Index and is described on [Naturindeksen](https://naturindeks.no/Indicators/ulv).

2. **Methods**:
The wolf population size is estimated from annual winter monitoring of tracks and signs in the field, which allows Rovdata to document the number of territory-holding wolves with wholly Norwegian residency and wolves with residency on both sides of the Norwegian–Swedish border [@rovdata_ulv_status_2025].
=======
<!--# 
With a maximum of 300 words, describe the indicator in general terms as you would to a non-expert. Think of this as a kind of common language summary. Follow the four numbers sub-headers below. under point 2, it is possible under  include a bullet point list of the specific steps in the workflow. 
 -->

1. **Background and rationale**:
The grey wolf (_Canis lupus_) is the largest member of the dog family and a social species; most wolves live in pairs or packs that defend territories.
In Norway the species is found mainly in southeastern parts of the country along the border with Sweden, although individual wolves can roam over long distances and may in principle occur anywhere on the mainland.
The species also occurs in large parts of Europe, Asia, and North America.
It is listed as critically endangered (kritisk truet) on the Norwegian Red List (2021).
Rovdata is responsible for monitoring wolves in Norway.
The indicator is part of the Norwegian Nature Index and is described on [Naturindeksen](https://naturindeks.no/Indicators/ulv).

2. **Methods**:
The wolf population size is estimated from ....
>>>>>>> 696d3711 (update wolf: new ID name, upscaling method and figure)
This value is then normalised based on a reference level, which is an expert-assessed population estimate based on known breeding density and the amount of suitable habitat.
The indicator is developed in the work with the Norwegian Nature Index [@nater_naturindeks_2025]. The methods are are described in full on the [Nature index web site](https://www.naturindeks.no/Indicators/ulv). 


3. **Interpretation**:
An indicator value of 1 means that the wolf population in the reporting unit is at least as large as expected under the reference condition used in Naturindeksen.
Values decreasing toward zero indicate that the population index is further below that reference.

4. **Results and development needs**:
<<<<<<< HEAD
For the current reporting period (2020-2024) the national indicator value is 0.03, with the highest value for region East (0.16), the lowest value for region Central (0.01), while all the other regions are 0.00.
The national indicator value has increased a little from 0.00 in 1990, with a peak in 2019 (0.04). 
For regions West, South, and North values have been at minimum since 1990. 
For the East values have increased steadily since 1990 and in the Central since 2019. 
There are no specific development needs, except for a possible review of reference values and the scaling function. 
=======
The indicator is used in Naturindeksen.
>>>>>>> 696d3711 (update wolf: new ID name, upscaling method and figure)


## 1.2 Native language summary - Norwegian

<<<<<<< HEAD
1. **Bakgrunn og begrunnelse**: Ulven (*Canis lupus*) er en tilpasningsdyktig art som trives i et mangfold av miljøer, inkludert skogøkosystemer [@ciucci2026wolf].
Som en toppredator påvirker ulven hjorteviltets atferd og utbredelse; ved å potensielt redusere antallet hjortevilt utøver den en «top-down»-kontroll som kan dempe beitetrykket og dermed fremme gjenoppretting av vegetasjon.
I Norge forekommer arten i dag i lave antall – noe som i stor grad skyldes en streng soneforvaltning der det brukes lisensfelling og aktivt uttak for å hindre arten i å etablere seg utenfor ulvesonen – og finnes hovedsakelig i de sørøstlige delene av landet langs grensen mot Sverige.
Indikatoren inngår i Naturindeksen og er beskrevet på [Naturindeksen](https://naturindeks.no/Indicators/ulv).

2. **Metoder**:
Ulvebestandens størrelse estimeres fra årlig vinterovervåking av spor og tegn i felt, som gjør det mulig for Rovdata å dokumentere antall revirkravende ulver med helst norsk tilhørighet og ulver med tilhørighet på begge sider av den norsk-svenske grensen [@rovdata_ulv_status_2025].
=======
1. **Bakgrunn og begrunnelse**: Ulv (_Canis lupus_) er det største medlemmet av hundefamilien og en sosial art; de fleste lever i par eller flokker som hevder territorier.
I Norge finnes arten hovedsakelig i sørøstlige deler av landet ved grensen mot Sverige, men enkeltdyr kan streife langt og kan i prinsippet dukke opp hvor som helst på fastlandet.
Arten finnes også i store deler av Europa, Asia og Nord-Amerika, og er vurdert som kritisk truet på Norsk rødliste (2021).
Rovdata har ansvar for overvåking av ulv i Norge.
Indikatoren inngår i Naturindeksen og er beskrevet på [Naturindeksen](https://naturindeks.no/Indicators/ulv).

2. **Metoder**: ...
>>>>>>> 696d3711 (update wolf: new ID name, upscaling method and figure)
Denne verdien normaliseres deretter basert på en referanseverdi, som er et ekspertvurdert bestandsestimat basert på kjent hekketetthet og mengden egnet habitat. Indikatoren er utviklet i arbeidet med Naturindeksen [@nater_naturindeks_2025]. Metodene er beskrevet i detalj på [Naturindeksens nettsider](https://www.naturindeks.no/Indicators/ulv).

3. **Tolkning**: En indikatorverdi på 1 betyr at ulvebestanden i rapporteringsenheten er minst like stor som forventet under referansetilstanden i Naturindeksen.
Verdier som nærmer seg null, viser at bestandsindekset ligger lenger under referansen.

<<<<<<< HEAD
4. **Resultater og utviklingsbehov**: For den nåværende rapporteringsperioden (2020–2024) er den nasjonale indikatoren 0,03, med høyest verdi for region Øst (0,16) og lavest verdi for region Midt (0,01), mens alle de øvrige regionene er 0,00.
Den nasjonale indikatoren har økt litt fra 0,00 i 1990, med en topp i 2019 (0,04).
For regionene Vest, Sør og Nord har verdiene ligget på minimum siden 1990.
For Øst har verdiene økt jevnt siden 1990, og for Midt siden 2019.
Det er ingen spesifikke utviklingsbehov, med mulig unntak for en gjennomgang av referanseverdier og skaleringsfunksjonen.
=======
4. **Resultater og utviklingsbehov**: Indikatoren brukes i Naturindeksen.
>>>>>>> 696d3711 (update wolf: new ID name, upscaling method and figure)


## 2. About the underlying data

<<<<<<< HEAD
The primary data source for this indicator is the national winter monitoring programme for wolves in Norway, coordinated by [Rovdata](https://rovdata.no/Ulv.aspx) under the large-carnivore monitoring framework and reported annually [@rovdata_ulv_status_2025].
Monitoring is based on field registration of tracks and signs during winter, from which the number of territory-holding wolves with wholly Norwegian or transboundary (Norwegian–Swedish) residency is determined.
Prepared indicator objects (population estimates or indices with uncertainty, plus reference values) are obtained from the Naturindeks database or equivalent exports.
=======
The data comes from ...
The procedure to download the data is described [here](https://naturindeks.nina.no/docs/24dda0ba-5855-4131-9168-d874980ec6aa.pdf). 
>>>>>>> 696d3711 (update wolf: new ID name, upscaling method and figure)

### 2.1 Spatial and temporal resolution and extent

Indicator values are available for the assessment years in Naturindeksen: 1990, 2000, 2010, 2014, 2019, and 2024.

To produce indicator estimates for these years, input is combined over a short window (typically two to three years), as specified in the Naturindeks indicator protocol.

Spatially, data are organised by the eight large-carnivore management regions (_forvaltningsregioner for rovvilt_, regions 1–8).
In Naturindeksen, indicator and reference values are stored at management-region scale (IC units), then aggregated to five geographic regions and national level for reporting, using the same spatial framework as for brown bear and lynx in this repository.

### 2.2 Sampling design

[Rovdata](https://rovdata.no/Ulv.aspx) is responsible for monitoring wolves in Norway and for maintaining an overview of how many wolves there are in the country.
Populations are estimated from annual winter monitoring, including counts of wolves with wholly Norwegian residency and wolves with residency on both sides of the border with Sweden, and reporting of wolf litters in Norwegian and border territories relative to national targets [@rovdata_ulv_status_2025].
Further detail on field methods (for example registration of tracks and signs) is linked from [Rovdata](https://rovdata.no/Ulv.aspx).

### 2.3 Original units

At the monitoring stage, Rovdata reports population status using numbers of wolves (including ranges for a given winter) and documentation of litters in territories [@rovdata_ulv_status_2025].

### 2.4 Instructions for citing, using and accessing data

Population monitoring and status information: cite [Rovdata](https://rovdata.no/Ulv.aspx) and the relevant status pages [@rovdata_ulv_status_2025].
Methodological documentation for the indicator: [Naturindeksen – ulv](https://naturindeks.no/Indicators/ulv).

<<<<<<< HEAD
=======
<!-- ### 2.5 Additional comments about the dataset -->

<!--# Optional. Text here -->
>>>>>>> 696d3711 (update wolf: new ID name, upscaling method and figure)

## 3. Indicator properties

### 3.1 Ecosystem Condition Typology Class (ECT)

<<<<<<< HEAD
Large carnivores function as apex predators, regulating prey populations and potentially exerting cascading effects on vegetation composition and the recruitment of tree species. 
Hence, this indicator is considered to fall under B3 Functional state characteristics. 
=======
B3 – Functional state characteristics.

<!-- 
### 3.1.1 Other condition typologies -->

<!--# Optional: Add text about other standards or typologies, such as alternative national condition typologies, and how the indicator relates to these -->
>>>>>>> 696d3711 (update wolf: new ID name, upscaling method and figure)


### 3.2 Ecosystem condition characteristic

<<<<<<< HEAD

### 3.3 Collinearities with other indicators

The indicator for wolf may covary with other large-carnivore indicators (brown bear, lynx) where distributions overlap.
In a natural system this would primarily reflect shared prey availability.
In contemporary Norway, however, population levels of all large carnivores are primarily determined by management rather than ecological dynamics. 

### 3.4 Impact factors

Natural factors regulating wolf population size include prey biomass availability (the primary constraint), habitat carrying capacity, and intraspecific mechanisms such as territoriality.
In Norway, these are largely overridden by anthropogenic ones: a strict zoning policy employs legal lethal removal to prevent establishment outside designated areas, while poaching exerts an additional – though poorly quantified – impact on overall population size. 
=======
<!--# 

Describe the ecosystem condition characteristic represented in the indicator. See 10.3897/oneeco.6.e58218 for information on what these characteristics might be.
For example, and indicator called 'Trenching in mires' could be made to represent an ecosystem characteristic 'Intact hydrology'. The term 'characteristic' is used similar to the term 'criteria' in Multiple Criteria Decision Making.  

-->


### 3.3 Collinearities with other indicators

<!--# Describe known collinearities with other metrics (indicators or variables) that could become problematic if they were also included in the same Ecosystem Condition Assessment as the indicator described here. -->

The indicator may covary with other large-carnivore indicators that use the same regional structure (brown bear, lynx, wolverine) where distributions overlap.

### 3.4 Impact factors

<!--# Describe the main natural and anthropogenic factors that affecst the metric -->
>>>>>>> 696d3711 (update wolf: new ID name, upscaling method and figure)


### 3.5 Spatial aggregation

<<<<<<< HEAD
Following the metadata pathway Aggregate (population model, large-carnivore management region) – [Scale – Truncate] – Downscale (municipalities) – Aggregate (geographic region) – Aggregate (national level): population estimates or indices are available per forvaltningsregion for rovvilt (IC unit in Naturindeksen). Each unit is normalised against _X~0~_ and _X~100~_ for that region (linear scaling with truncation to [0, 1]).

Because rovvilt regions and the five Naturindeks geographic regions are two non-aligned partitions of Norway, we use municipalities (`BSunits`) as a common basic spatial unit. We assign the (scaled) rovvilt-region value to each municipality belonging to that region (uniform-value assumption), hereafter referred to as downscaling. Forest area (`Skog`) is used as the aggregation weight because wolf is a forest-associated species.
=======
<!-- Describe in word the same aggregation pathway presented in the top yaml (the metadata). If you have scaled against the reference value, it is especially important to know if you did any avergaing or summations etc. beforehand. An example text for a rather simple case could be: "The variable is scaled at the initial polygon level and the point estimate is a area-weighted arithmetic mean of all polygins. There is no transformation, and truncation is not needed as the indicator becomes naturally bound between 0 and 1".  -->

Following the metadata pathway Aggregate (population model, large-carnivore management region) – [Scale – Truncate] – Aggregate (national level): values are handled per forvaltningsregion for rovvilt in Naturindeksen, then aggregated to five geographic regions and national level with area weights (see Section 8).
Each unit is normalised against _X~0~_ and _X~100~_ for that region (linear scaling with truncation to [0, 1]).
>>>>>>> 696d3711 (update wolf: new ID name, upscaling method and figure)

## 4. Reference condition and levels

### 4.1 Reference condition

<<<<<<< HEAD
The reference condition for the wolf represents the natural population state occurring in the absence of significant human influence, where the species thrives across diverse environments including forest and woodland ecosystems.
In this state, population size, structure, and dynamics are regulated primarily by natural processes – including prey biomass availability, which acts as the ultimate regulating factor, habitat carrying capacity, and intraspecific social mechanisms such as territoriality and dispersal.
The population fulfills its natural functional role as a cursorial apex predator – meaning it is biologically adapted for running and chasing prey over long distances – thereby influencing the ecosystem through predation on ungulates. 

Wolves influence the behavior, distribution, and vigilance of herbivores, potentially triggering trophic cascades that reduce browsing pressure and allow for the recovery of vegetation and associated biodiversity.
Furthermore, the wolf structures interactions at the community level by suppressing mesopredators and by acting as a keystone provider for a diverse guild of scavengers through the consistent provisioning of carcasses.
Population size naturally varies within dynamic ranges in response to climatic variation, which affects prey vulnerability, and complex interactions with competing apex predators such as the brown bear.
This condition corresponds to the maximum ecological integrity of the ecosystem. 
=======
The reference condition for wolf in Naturindeksen is a boreal/montane forest ecosystem with minimal human impact and a climate similar to the reference period used in the Norwegian Nature Index framework [@nybo_fagsystem_2017].
>>>>>>> 696d3711 (update wolf: new ID name, upscaling method and figure)

### 4.2 Reference levels

_X~100~_ is set per large-carnivore management region (IC unit in Naturindeksen), using suitable area and density within each _rovviltregion_ and expert assessment: it represents the expected wolf population (or equivalent index) under reference conditions in that unit.

_X~0~_ is defined as no wolves in the unit (functional absence).


#### 4.2.1 Spatial resolution and validity

<!--# 

Describe the spatial resolution of the reference levels. E.g. is it defined as a fixed value for all areas, or does it vary. Also, at what spatial scale are the reference levels valid? For example, if the reference levels have a regional resolution (varies between regions), it might mean that it is only valid and correct to use for normalising local variable values that are first aggregated to regional scale. However, sometimes the reference levels are insensitive to this and can be used to scale variables at the local (e.g. plot) scale. 

 -->

Reference levels and indicator values apply at the scale of forvaltningsregioner for rovvilt (IC units).
Aggregated results for the five Naturindeks geographic regions and for Norway are summaries of those units with area weights.

## 5. Uncertainties
### 5.1 General uncertainties

Reference levels are held fixed in this workflow (their own uncertainties are not propagated).
Regional and national values are area-weighted summaries of forvaltningsregioner for rovvilt, so they inherit model uncertainty at management-region scale and any mismatch between administrative regions and ecological variation within the five Naturindeks geographic regions.
Monitoring design and sample effort differ between species and regions; uneven coverage can affect the population models that enter Naturindeksen.

### 5.2 Reported uncertainties

<<<<<<< HEAD
The intervals in @tbl-wolf-results-regional, @tbl-wolf-results-national, @fig-wolf-norm, and @fig-wolf-timeSeries are inferential: they describe uncertainty around the estimated area-weighted mean indicator for each reporting unit (a geographic region or Norway as a whole), given the statistical models used for management-region abundance in Naturindeksen.

For each management region and assessment year, Naturindeks provides a predictive distribution for the population index (on the variable scale before scaling).
We draw `nsim = 1000` values per region, scale each draw to the 0–1 indicator scale using the regional reference level and linear scaling, then downscale by assigning each scaled draw to every municipality in that region and aggregate with `ecTools::ec_upscale()`: first from municipalities (`BSunits`) to each NI geographic region (`part`), then from regions to Norway, using forest area (`Skog`) as the weight.
The 1000 `sampled_mean` values per region and year describe inferential uncertainty for the forest-area-weighted mean indicator; we summarise them with the median and 2.5% and 97.5% quantiles as an approximate 95% interval, and the 25% and 75% quantiles as an approximate interquartile range.
=======
The intervals in @tbl-wolf-results, @fig-wolf-norm, and @fig-wolf-timeSeries are inferential: they describe uncertainty around the estimated area-weighted mean indicator for each reporting unit (a geographic region or Norway as a whole), given the statistical models used for management-region abundance in Naturindeksen.

For each management region and assessment year, Naturindeks provides a predictive distribution for the population index (on the variable scale before scaling).
We draw `nsim = 1000` values per region, scale each draw to the 0–1 indicator scale using the regional reference level and linear scaling, then aggregate with `ecTools::ec_upscale()`: first from management regions to each NI geographic region (`part`), then from regions to Norway, using summed terrestrial area as weights (same structure as forest-area weighting for the cervid indicators).
The 1000 `sampled_mean` values per region and year describe inferential uncertainty for the area-weighted mean indicator; we summarise them with the median and 2.5% and 97.5% quantiles as an approximate 95% interval, and the 25% and 75% quantiles as an approximate interquartile range.
>>>>>>> 696d3711 (update wolf: new ID name, upscaling method and figure)


## 6. References

::: {#refs}
:::

## 7. Import and prepare datasets

Run the download chunk once to refresh the local export. This project’s renv environment installs `NIcalc` from GitHub `ChloeRN/NIcalc`, branch `include_ReadOnly_access` (see `renv.lock` and `install_deps.R`); that build defaults to the Naturindeks read-only API user, so you do not need `username` / `password` arguments here. When [NINAnor/NIcalc PR #59](https://github.com/NINAnor/NIcalc/pull/59) is merged into `NINAnor/NIcalc`, you can switch the renv install back to the main repo and update the lockfile.
We save the RDS export under `data/` so that we do not need to download the data every time we run the analysis.

```{r wolf-import-api}
#| code-summary: "Download wolf data from Naturindeks API"
#| eval: false

wolfImport <- NIcalc::importDatasetApi(
  eco = NULL,
  indic = c("Ulv"),
  year = c("1990", "2000", "2010", "2014", "2019", "2024")
)

saveRDS(wolfImport, here::here("data/wolfImport.rds"))
```

```{r wolf-load-import}
#| include: false
#| cache: false
#| code-summary: "Load saved Naturindeks import"

wolfImport <- readRDS(here::here("data/wolfImport.rds"))
```


## 8. Spatial units

<<<<<<< HEAD
According to Naturindeks terminology, municipalities are `BSunits`.
The eight forvaltningsregioner for rovvilt (rovvilt regions 1–8) are subsamples of `BSunits` and are referred to as `ICunits` in the import; they are the IC units for this indicator.
The five Naturindeks geographic regions (east, south, west, central, north; codes E, S, W, C, N) are `parts` and `NIunits`, taken from the `part` field on municipality-level `BSunits` in the same import (see Section 9 for downscaling and aggregation).
Mainland Norway is the ecosystem accounting area.

```{r fig-wolf-carnivoreRegions}
#| fig-cap: "Map of the eight carnivore management regions (rovvilt regions) in Norway."
knitr::include_graphics(here::here("img/rovviltregioner.png"))
```

## 9. Analyses

This section uses the following workflow: we take variable values and reference levels from the Naturindeks import, normalise at large-carnivore management-region scale (IC unit) with linear scaling, then downscale by assigning the scaled values to the municipalities in each region and propagate uncertainty and aggregate spatially with forest-area weights using `ecTools::ec_upscale()`.
At geographic (NI part) and national scale, uncertainties are inferential: each replicate is a forest-area-weighted mean across municipalities (then, for Norway, one draw per NI region).
We use linear scaling and forest area (`Skog`) from the Naturindeks spatial framework as the weight.
=======
The eight forvaltningsregioner for rovvilt (rovvilt regions 1–8) are the IC units in Naturindeks for this indicator.
The five Naturindeks geographic regions (east, south, west, central, north; codes E, S, W, C, N) are taken from the `part` field on municipality-level `BSunits` in the same import, aggregated up to each management region (see Section 9).
Mainland Norway is the ecosystem accounting area.

## 9. Analyses

This section uses the following workflow: we take variable values and reference levels from the Naturindeks import, normalise at large-carnivore management-region scale (IC unit) with linear scaling, then propagate uncertainty and aggregate spatially with terrestrial area weights using `ecTools::ec_upscale()`.
At geographic (NI part) and national scale, uncertainties are inferential: each replicate is a terrestrial-area-weighted mean based on one draw per management region (then, for Norway, one draw per NI region).
We use linear scaling and summed terrestrial area from the Naturindeks spatial framework as weights (analogous to forest area in the cervid indicators).
>>>>>>> 696d3711 (update wolf: new ID name, upscaling method and figure)

Management-region population indices in the database are stored as predictive distributions, not only as point estimates.
Reference levels per region (_X~0~_ = 0, _X~100~_ from the download) are applied before any geographic or national aggregation.
We ignore uncertainty in the reference levels themselves.

### Assemble import and indicator object

`assembleNiObject()` reshapes `wolfImport` so that indicator observations are in one list element per assessment year, and so that underlying spatial units (`BSunits`) are aligned with the thematic index structure used in Naturindeks.

```{r wolf-assemble}
#| code-summary: "Assemble wolf NI object (thematic, predefined regions)"
wolfAssemble <- NIcalc::assembleNiObject(
  inputData = wolfImport,
  predefNIunits = c(allArea = T, parts = T, counties = F),
  indexType = "thematic"
)
```

### Reference levels (X~0~ and X~100~)

Before scaling, we read regional reference levels from `referenceValues` in the RDS export (rows with `yearId == 0` or reference year name).
For this indicator, _X~0~_ is zero individuals in the unit and _X~100~_ is the expert reference population index per rovvilt region (`expectedValue` in the reference slice).

```{r wolf-refs}
#| code-summary: "Management-region reference levels from downloaded Naturindeks data"
#| tbl-cap: "Management-region reference levels (X0 = 0, X100) by rovvilt region (IC unit)."
<<<<<<< HEAD
refs <- wolfImport$referenceValues$referenceValues |> 
=======
refs <- wolfImport$referenceValues$referenceValues |>
>>>>>>> 696d3711 (update wolf: new ID name, upscaling method and figure)
  dplyr::filter(.data$yearId == 0L | grepl("Referanse", .data$yearName, fixed = FALSE)) |>
  dplyr::distinct(ICunitId, ICunitName, x100 = expectedValue) |>
  dplyr::mutate(x0 = 0)
refs |>
  gt::gt()
```

### Resample non-scaled management-region variables

For each assessment year, Naturindeks stores one value per rovvilt region as a parameterised distribution (or a custom distribution).
We draw `nsim = 1000` random values per region with `NIcalc::sampleObsMat()`.
Rows with a standard distribution family use observation type `tradObs`; rows with only a custom distribution use `customObs`.

Assessment years are 1990, 2000, 2010, 2014, 2019, and 2024.
The result `mgmt_draws` is a long table of simulated population indices (`value`) by `ICunitId` and `year`, not yet on the 0–1 indicator scale.

```{r wolf-vars}
#| code-summary: "Observation types for sampleObsMat"
myYears <- as.character(c(1990, 2000, 2010, 2014, 2019, 2024))
obstype <- vector("list", length(myYears))
for (i in seq_along(myYears)) {
  obs <- wolfAssemble$indicatorValues[[i]]$distributionFamilyName
  obs[!is.na(obs)] <- "tradObs"
  obs[is.na(obs)] <- "customObs"
  obstype[[i]] <- obs
}
```

```{r wolf-draw}
#| code-summary: "Resample management-region variables (non-scaled)"
#| tbl-cap: "Preview of resampled management-region variable values (first rows)."
#| warning: false
#| message: false
mgmt_draws <- tibble(ICunitId = NULL, value = NULL, year = NULL)

for (i in seq_along(myYears)) {
  myMat <- NIcalc::sampleObsMat(
    ICunitId = wolfAssemble$indicatorValues[[i]]$ICunitId,
    value = wolfAssemble$indicatorValues[[i]]$expectedValue,
    distrib = wolfAssemble$indicatorValues[[i]]$distributionFamilyName,
    mu = wolfAssemble$indicatorValues[[i]]$distParameter1,
    sig = wolfAssemble$indicatorValues[[i]]$distParameter2,
    customDistribution = wolfAssemble$indicatorValues[[i]]$customDistribution,
    obsType = obstype[[i]],
    nsim = 1000
  )
  ICunitIds <- row.names(myMat)
  colnames(myMat) <- paste0("s", seq_len(ncol(myMat)))
  myMat <- myMat |>
    as_tibble() |>
    add_column(ICunitId = ICunitIds, .before = 1) |>
    pivot_longer(cols = -any_of("ICunitId"), names_to = "sim_col", values_to = "value") |>
    mutate(
      sim_id = readr::parse_number(.data$sim_col),
      year = myYears[i]
    ) |>
    select(ICunitId, sim_id, value, year)

  mgmt_draws <- mgmt_draws |>
    bind_rows(myMat)
}
head(mgmt_draws) |>
  gt::gt()
```

### Scaling to indicator values (linear)

Normalisation is done at management-region scale before any geographic or national aggregation.
Each draw is joined to its regional `x0` and `x100`, then mapped to `[0, 1]` with `ecTools::ec_normalise()` using linear increasing scaling from _X~0~_ to _X~100~_ (no upper-tail unimodal anchor).

```{r wolf-join-scale}
#| code-summary: "Join reference levels and linear scaling to indicator scale"
mgmt_draws <- mgmt_draws |>
  left_join(
    refs |> mutate(ICunitId = as.character(ICunitId)),
    by = join_by(ICunitId)
  )

ind <- ec_normalise(
  variable = mgmt_draws$value,
  x0 = mgmt_draws$x0,
  x100 = mgmt_draws$x100,
  fun = "linear"
)

mgmt_draws <- mgmt_draws |> add_column(indicator = ind)
```

The scatter plot below (@fig-wolf-norm) shows the mapping from variable to indicator for each rovvilt region (one facet per `ICunitName`). The figure is saved when the document is rendered.

```{r wolf-normplot}
#| eval: true
<<<<<<< HEAD
#| code-summary: "Save normalisation diagnostic figure"
wolf_norm_plot <- ec_norm_plot(mgmt_draws$value, mgmt_draws$indicator) +
  facet_wrap(. ~ mgmt_draws$ICunitName) +
  ggplot2::theme(axis.text = ggplot2::element_text(size = 9))
wolf_norm_plot$layers[[1]]$aes_params$size <- 3

suppressMessages(
  ggsave(
    filename = here::here("img/wolf_norm_plot.png"),
    plot = wolf_norm_plot,
    scale = 1.5
  )
)

=======
#| code-summary: "Save normalisation diagnostic figure (English)"
wolf_plot <- ec_norm_plot(mgmt_draws$value, mgmt_draws$indicator) +
  facet_wrap(. ~ mgmt_draws$ICunitName)
ggsave(filename = here::here("img/wolf_norm_plot_eng.png"), plot = wolf_plot, scale = 1.5)
>>>>>>> 696d3711 (update wolf: new ID name, upscaling method and figure)
```

```{r fig-wolf-norm}
#| fig-cap: "Linear normalisation of grey wolf population index to indicator values (one panel per management region)."
<<<<<<< HEAD
knitr::include_graphics(here::here("img/wolf_norm_plot.png"))
```

### Aggregation weights (forest area by municipality)

Spatial aggregation uses forest area (`Skog`) on each underlying municipality in `BSunits`.
Municipalities are linked to rovvilt regions via `ICunits`, and each `BSunit` carries a Naturindeks geographic region code (`part`: E, S, W, C, N).
Rovvilt regions and NI parts are two non-aligned partitions of Norway, so we keep the data at municipality level once it is scaled: each municipality inherits the scaled indicator value of its rovvilt region (uniform-value assumption), and is then aggregated upward with its own forest area.

```{r wolf-weights}
#| code-summary: "Forest area, NI part, and IC link per municipality"
=======
knitr::include_graphics(here::here("img/wolf_norm_plot_eng.png"))
```

### Aggregation weights (terrestrial area by management region)

Spatial aggregation uses terrestrial area on each underlying spatial unit in `BSunits`, summed to management region (`ICunitId`).
Municipalities are linked to rovvilt regions via `ICunits`, as for counties in the cervid indicators.
Each `BSunit` carries a Naturindeks geographic region code (`part`: E, S, W, C, N).
We sum terrestrial area by management region and by `part`, assign each management region to the `part` with the largest area share, and use total terrestrial area per management region as the weight in geographic and national means.

```{r wolf-weights}
#| code-summary: "Terrestrial area and NI part from BSunits in the import"
>>>>>>> 696d3711 (update wolf: new ID name, upscaling method and figure)
mgmt_weights <- as_tibble(wolfAssemble$BSunits) |>
  rename(BSunitId = id)

IC_BC_link <- wolfImport$ICunits |>
  distinct(ICunitId = as.character(id), BSunitId)

mgmt_weights <- mgmt_weights |>
<<<<<<< HEAD
  left_join(IC_BC_link, by = join_by(BSunitId)) |>
  mutate(ICunitId = as.character(ICunitId))
```

Downscale the scaled indicator values from each rovvilt region by assigning them to every municipality in that region.
The result is a long table with one row per (`year`, `sim_id`, `BSunitId`) carrying its rovvilt region's indicator draw, its `part`, and the municipality's `Skog`.

```{r wolf-downscale}
#| code-summary: "Downscale rovvilt-region draws to municipalities"
mgmt_draws <- mgmt_draws |>
  mutate(ICunitId = as.character(ICunitId))

bsunit_draws <- mgmt_draws |>
  right_join(
    mgmt_weights |> select(BSunitId, ICunitId, part, Skog),
    by = join_by(ICunitId),
    unmatched = "error",
    relationship = "many-to-many"
  )
=======
  left_join(IC_BC_link, by = join_by(BSunitId))

ic_names <- wolfAssemble$indicatorValues[[1]] |>
  transmute(ICunitId = as.character(ICunitId), ICunitName)

part_from_data <- mgmt_weights |>
  group_by(ICunitId, part) |>
  summarise(area_in_part = sum(terrestrialArea), .groups = "drop") |>
  group_by(ICunitId) |>
  slice_max(area_in_part, n = 1, with_ties = FALSE) |>
  select(ICunitId, part)

mgmt_weights <- mgmt_weights |>
  group_by(ICunitId) |>
  summarise(terrestrial_area = sum(terrestrialArea), .groups = "drop") |>
  left_join(part_from_data, by = join_by(ICunitId)) |>
  left_join(ic_names, by = join_by(ICunitId))

mgmt_draws <- mgmt_draws |>
  left_join(
    mgmt_weights |> select(ICunitId, terrestrial_area, part),
    by = join_by(ICunitId)
  ) |>
  mutate(ICunitId = as.character(ICunitId))
>>>>>>> 696d3711 (update wolf: new ID name, upscaling method and figure)
```

### Spatial aggregation

<<<<<<< HEAD
Aggregate from municipalities to NI geographic regions with `ecTools::ec_upscale()`, using Monte Carlo propagation of inferential uncertainty (one draw per municipality per replicate, forest-area-weighted mean).

```{r wolf-aggregate1}
#| code-summary: "Municipality to NI part MC propagation (ec_upscale)"
=======
Aggregate from management regions to NI geographic regions with `ecTools::ec_upscale()`, using Monte Carlo propagation of inferential uncertainty (one draw per management region per replicate, terrestrial-area-weighted mean).

```{r wolf-aggregate1}
#| code-summary: "Management region to NI part MC propagation (ec_upscale)"
>>>>>>> 696d3711 (update wolf: new ID name, upscaling method and figure)
#| eval: false
#| warning: false
#| message: false
mc_propagated <- ecTools::ec_upscale(
<<<<<<< HEAD
  data = bsunit_draws,
  variable = indicator,
  weight = Skog,
  start_units = BSunitId,
=======
  data = mgmt_draws,
  variable = indicator,
  weight = terrestrial_area,
  start_units = ICunitId,
>>>>>>> 696d3711 (update wolf: new ID name, upscaling method and figure)
  end_units = part,
  year = year,
  end_units_name = "part",
  n = 1000
)
saveRDS(mc_propagated, here::here("data/wolf_mc_propagated_regional.rds"))
```

```{r wolf-load-mc-regional}
#| include: false
mc_propagated <- readRDS(here::here("data/wolf_mc_propagated_regional.rds"))
head(mc_propagated)
```

For the national indicator, propagate again from regions to Norway.
<<<<<<< HEAD
Summed forest area per geographic region is used as the weight at this step.

```{r wolf-region-weights}
#| code-summary: "Total forest area per NI region"
region_weights <- mgmt_weights |>
  group_by(part) |>
  summarise(forest_area = sum(.data$Skog, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop")
=======
Summed terrestrial area per geographic region is used as weight at this step.

```{r wolf-region-weights}
#| code-summary: "Total terrestrial area per NI region"
region_weights <- mgmt_weights |>
  group_by(part) |>
  summarise(terrestrial_area = sum(.data$terrestrial_area), .groups = "drop")
>>>>>>> 696d3711 (update wolf: new ID name, upscaling method and figure)
region_weights
```

```{r wolf-agg-national}
#| code-summary: "NI region to Norway MC propagation (ec_upscale)"
#| eval: false
#| warning: false
#| message: false
mc_propagated_for_national <- mc_propagated |>
  left_join(region_weights, by = join_by(part)) |>
  add_column(end_units = "Norway")

mc_propagated_national <- ecTools::ec_upscale(
  data = mc_propagated_for_national,
  variable = sampled_mean,
<<<<<<< HEAD
  weight = forest_area,
=======
  weight = terrestrial_area,
>>>>>>> 696d3711 (update wolf: new ID name, upscaling method and figure)
  start_units = part,
  end_units = end_units,
  year = year,
  end_units_name = "part",
  n = 1000
)
saveRDS(mc_propagated_national, here::here("data/wolf_mc_propagated_national.rds"))
```

```{r wolf-load-mc-national}
#| include: false
mc_propagated_national <- readRDS(here::here("data/wolf_mc_propagated_national.rds"))
head(mc_propagated_national)
```

Regional and national MC draws are combined for summarisation and export.

```{r wolf-mc-combined}
#| code-summary: "Bind regional and national MC outputs"
mc_propagated_combined <- mc_propagated |>
  select(year, part, sampled_mean) |>
  bind_rows(mc_propagated_national)
```

## 10. Results

<<<<<<< HEAD
From the 1000 Monte Carlo replicates per region and year in `mc_propagated_combined`, we report the median as the point estimate and use the 2.5% and 97.5% quantiles as an approximate 95% interval for the forest-area-weighted mean indicator (inferential uncertainty, not descriptive spread between management regions).
The tables (@tbl-wolf-results-regional, @tbl-wolf-results-national) and time series (@fig-wolf-timeSeries) summarise regional and national (`Norway`) values separately.
=======
From the 1000 Monte Carlo replicates per region and year in `mc_propagated_combined`, we report the median as the point estimate and use the 2.5% and 97.5% quantiles as an approximate 95% interval for the terrestrial-area-weighted mean indicator (inferential uncertainty, not descriptive spread between management regions).
The table (@tbl-wolf-results) and time series (@fig-wolf-timeSeries) summarise regional and national (`Norway`) values.
>>>>>>> 696d3711 (update wolf: new ID name, upscaling method and figure)

```{r wolf-part-labels}
#| include: false
wolf_part_labels <- c(
  N = "North",
  C = "Central",
  W = "West",
  S = "South",
  E = "East",
  Norway = "Norway",
  All = "Norway"
)
wolf_region_levels <- c("Norway", "Central", "East", "North", "South", "West")
```

```{r wolf-summaryStats}
#| warning: false
#| message: false
#| cache: false
#| tbl-cap: "Wolf indicator summaries by region and year (inferential uncertainty for area-weighted means)."
summary_out <- mc_propagated_combined |>
  mutate(part = dplyr::recode(part, !!!wolf_part_labels)) |>
  group_by(part, year) |>
  summarise(
    median = round(median(sampled_mean, na.rm = TRUE), 2),
    p_025 = round(quantile(sampled_mean, probs = 0.025, na.rm = TRUE), 2),
    p_25 = round(quantile(sampled_mean, probs = 0.25, na.rm = TRUE), 2),
    p_75 = round(quantile(sampled_mean, probs = 0.75, na.rm = TRUE), 2),
    p_975 = round(quantile(sampled_mean, probs = 0.975, na.rm = TRUE), 2),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) |>
  mutate(part = factor(part, levels = wolf_region_levels)) |>
  add_column(
    ECT = "B3",
    Indicator = "Wolf abundance",
    Ecosystem = "Boreal/montane forests",
    ID = "NO_WOLF_001"
  )
```

```{r fig-wolf-timeSeries}
#| cache: false
#| fig-cap: "Indicator values for wolf abundance by region and nationally, with uncertainty bands from the resampled distribution."
wolf_plot_years <- as.character(c(1990, 2000, 2010, 2014, 2019, 2024))
wolf_region_cols <- setNames(
  MetBrewer::met.brewer("Archambault", n = length(wolf_region_levels)),
  wolf_region_levels
)

plot_data <- summary_out |>
  mutate(
    year = factor(year, levels = wolf_plot_years),
    series_type = if_else(part == "Norway", "National", "Region")
  )

<<<<<<< HEAD
<<<<<<< HEAD
=======
>>>>>>> 1a3e7bdf (change yaxis on temporal figure)
y_hi <- max(plot_data$p_975, na.rm = TRUE)
y_max <- min(1, max(0.08, y_hi * 1.12, na.rm = TRUE))
y_breaks <- pretty(c(0, y_max), n = 6)

<<<<<<< HEAD
=======
>>>>>>> 696d3711 (update wolf: new ID name, upscaling method and figure)
=======
>>>>>>> 1a3e7bdf (change yaxis on temporal figure)
ggplot(plot_data, aes(x = year, y = median, colour = part, group = part)) +
  geom_line(
    aes(linewidth = series_type),
    position = position_dodge(width = 0.18)
  ) +
  geom_pointrange(
    aes(ymin = p_025, ymax = p_975, shape = series_type),
    size = 0.8,
    linewidth = 0.45,
    position = position_dodge(width = 0.18)
  ) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = wolf_region_cols, name = NULL) +
  scale_shape_manual(
    values = c(National = 19, Region = 17),
    name = NULL
  ) +
  scale_linewidth_manual(
    values = c(National = 1, Region = 0.55),
    guide = "none"
  ) +
<<<<<<< HEAD
<<<<<<< HEAD
=======
>>>>>>> 1a3e7bdf (change yaxis on temporal figure)
  scale_y_continuous(
    limits = c(0, y_max),
    breaks = y_breaks,
    expand = ggplot2::expansion(mult = c(0.02, 0.04))
  ) +
<<<<<<< HEAD
=======
  scale_y_continuous(limits = c(0, 1), breaks = seq(0, 1, 0.2)) +
>>>>>>> 696d3711 (update wolf: new ID name, upscaling method and figure)
=======
>>>>>>> 1a3e7bdf (change yaxis on temporal figure)
  theme_bw(base_size = 12) +
  theme(
    legend.position = "bottom",
    legend.box = "vertical",
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.major.x = element_blank()
  ) +
  guides(
    colour = guide_legend(nrow = 2, override.aes = list(size = 0.7, linewidth = 0.45)),
    shape = guide_legend(override.aes = list(size = 0.8, linewidth = 0.45))
  ) +
  labs(y = "Indicator value", x = "")
```

<<<<<<< HEAD
```{r tbl-wolf-results-regional}
#| warning: false
#| message: false
#| cache: false
#| tbl-cap: "Indicator values and uncertainty estimates for wolf abundance by geographic region."

summary_out |>
  filter(part != "Norway") |>
=======
```{r tbl-wolf-results}
#| warning: false
#| message: false
#| cache: false
#| tbl-cap: "Indicator values and uncertainty estimates for wolf abundance."

summary_out |>
>>>>>>> 696d3711 (update wolf: new ID name, upscaling method and figure)
  arrange(part, year) |>
  unite(col = "CI_95", c(p_025, p_975), sep = " - ") |>
  unite(col = "IQR", c(p_25, p_75), sep = " - ") |>
  select(
    ECT,
    Indicator,
    ID,
    "Accounting area" = part,
    Ecosystem,
    "Closing year" = year,
    "Indicator value" = median,
    "95% CI" = CI_95,
    "50% CI" = IQR
  ) |>
  kable()
```

<<<<<<< HEAD
```{r tbl-wolf-results-national}
#| warning: false
#| message: false
#| cache: false
#| tbl-cap: "Indicator values and uncertainty estimates for wolf abundance nationally (Norway)."

summary_out |>
  filter(part == "Norway") |>
  arrange(year) |>
  unite(col = "CI_95", c(p_025, p_975), sep = " - ") |>
  unite(col = "IQR", c(p_25, p_75), sep = " - ") |>
  select(
    ECT,
    Indicator,
    ID,
    "Accounting area" = part,
    Ecosystem,
    "Closing year" = year,
    "Indicator value" = median,
    "95% CI" = CI_95,
    "50% CI" = IQR
  ) |>
  kable()
```

=======
>>>>>>> 696d3711 (update wolf: new ID name, upscaling method and figure)
## 11. Export file

Regional and national Monte Carlo draws are exported in one table (`mc_propagated_combined`: `year`, `part`, `sampled_mean`; `part` includes NI regions and `Norway`).

```{r wolf-export}
#| eval: false
#| code-summary: "Export regional and national MC-propagated indicator draws"
write.csv(
  mc_propagated_combined,
  here::here("data/results_NO_WOLF_001_v1.0.0.csv"),
  row.names = FALSE
)
```

## 12. Author contributions

<<<<<<< HEAD
**CRediT statement**
 
* Conceptualization: [All]
* Methodology: [AHH]
* Formal analysis: [AHH]
* Writing – original draft: [AHH]
* Writing – review & editing: [All] 

 We would, also, like to acknowledge the work of those that created the indicator on _Naturindeks_ [@nater_naturindeks_2025].
=======
<!-- Fill in CReDIT and MeRIT statments to clarify the contributions from individual authors. For MeRIT, see https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-023-37039-1. For CRedit, see https://credit.niso.org/. You can start with the template structur below, and just delete the rows you dont use.  -->

Not relevant, as this is the work of a single author. I would, however, like to acknowledge the work of those that created the indicator on _Naturindeks_ [@nater_naturindeks_2025].
>>>>>>> 696d3711 (update wolf: new ID name, upscaling method and figure)

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
## Session Info
<!-- You can leave this last part as it is, unless you have a good reason to alter it. -->

This information is mainly aimed at developers that need to recreate the R environment that produced the html.
Important note: this information is about the R session that was running when the html was rendered, and if some of the code chunks were not evaluated when the html was rendered, they may have been executed in a different environment.  

```{r sessionInfo}
#| echo: false
sessionInfo()
```

:::
